quick_definition: The annualized, time-weighted rate that discounts a fund's or deal's cash flows to zero — PE's headline return number.
The absolute multiple capital returns — total value ÷ capital invested (e.g., 2.5×); ignores time.
The three multiples splitting fund value into cash already returned (DPI), value still held (RVPI), and the sum (TVPI).
The shape of an LP's cumulative net cash flow — negative early (fees + drawdowns), turning positive as exits return capital.
A benchmark comparing a fund's return to what the same cash flows would have earned in a public index.
The fund's manager — makes all investment decisions, owes a fiduciary duty to LPs, and bears unlimited liability.
A passive investor in the fund whose liability is capped at its committed capital.
The annual fee (~1.3–2.5% of committed, then invested, capital) paid to the manager to run the fund.
The standard PE comp model: ~2% management fee + 20% of profits (carry) to the GP.
The GP's ~20% share of net fund profits — the main performance incentive.
The contractual order proceeds flow back: return capital → preferred return → GP catch-up → split profits.
The minimum annual return (typically 8%) LPs must get before the GP earns carry.
The waterfall step letting the GP collect profits until it holds its full ~20% once the hurdle is met.
An LPA provision forcing the GP to return any carry it was overpaid across the fund's life.
The GP's demand for a portion of committed capital as deals and fees arise.
Capital pledged vs. capital actually called (paid-in) vs. capital deployed into deals.
Committed-but-not-yet-invested capital available to deploy (fund-level or industry-wide).
The year a fund first closes and starts investing — the basis for peer benchmarking.
A fund whose investors commit before knowing which companies it will buy, trusting the mandate.
The fund's governing contract — mandate, economics, governance, and LP–GP rights.
The ~4–5-year window during which the GP can call capital for new investments.
The ~3–7 years a fund owns a company, working to create value before exit.
The GP's own capital in the fund (typically 1–5%) that aligns it with LPs.
Earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation & amortization — the cash-earnings proxy PE prices and lends against.
The value of the whole business — what you'd pay to own its operations free of its capital structure.
What's left for shareholders after subtracting net debt from enterprise value.
Shorthand pricing ratios (EV/EBITDA, EV/Sales, P/E) that value a company relative to peers or precedents.
The systematic investigation of a target's business, finances, legal standing, and risks before committing.
Borrowed money used alongside equity to fund a deal, amplifying both returns and risk.
Total debt minus cash — the figure that bridges enterprise value to equity value.
The cash tied up in day-to-day operations; its "normal" level is pegged at closing.
The deal-funding table showing where money comes from (debt + equity) and what it pays for.
The layered mix of debt instruments ranked by seniority and risk (senior → mezzanine → PIK).
A condition in a loan agreement that protects lenders and constrains the borrower.
A holding company created to ring-fence a deal, house debt, and hold equity.
The master contract governing a company's sale — price, conditions, warranties, remedies.
Shares that rank ahead of common stock for distributions and carry special rights.
Preferred holders' right to get their money back (sometimes a multiple) before common in an exit.
Protections adjusting earlier investors' price if a later round is raised at a lower valuation.
The ledger of who owns what — shares, options, and ownership percentages.
A company's value before vs. after new investment (post = pre + new money).
The drop in existing owners' percentage stake when new shares are issued.
Rights letting a majority force, or a minority join, a sale of shares.
The protections a non-controlling investor negotiates to safeguard its stake and influence.
The outsized-upside equity package given to portfolio-company management.
The non-binding document setting the headline economic and control terms of an investment.
PE's hands-on, urgency-driven involvement in a company during the holding period.
Structuring incentives so management, GP, and LPs all win from the same outcome.
Growing returns by improving the actual business — revenue, margins, operations — not just leverage.
The urgent post-close action plan setting priorities, metrics, and quick wins from day one.
The board-level control and oversight structures PE uses to steer a portfolio company.
The sale or listing through which a fund realizes its investment and returns capital.
Listing the company publicly to sell the stake down over time.
Selling the company to a strategic (corporate) buyer.
Selling a portfolio company to another PE fund.
LPs investing directly alongside a GP in a specific deal, usually fee-free.
The market for buying/selling existing fund stakes and portfolios.
A loan's income return — cash coupon plus fees, expressed as cash yield, yield-to-maturity, or yield-to-3-year (assuming early take-out); credit's headline number, the way IRR is PE's.
The benchmark tracking the total return of U.S. middle-market directly-originated loans (BDC-sourced) — the asset class's published track record.
Annualized losses on a portfolio = default rate × loss-given-default; the drag that turns gross yield into net return.
How many cents on the dollar a lender recovers when a borrower defaults — driven by seniority, security, and enterprise value.
The share of borrowers that breach or fail to pay over a period — distinct from loss, since a default with full recovery costs nothing.
The structural shift of corporate lending from regulated banks to asset managers — the force that created the asset class.
A U.S.-regulated, tax-pass-through (RIC) vehicle built to lend to private middle-market companies — the dominant access point for direct lending.
The band of companies (segmented lower / core / upper by EBITDA) that private credit principally serves — too big for bank relationships, too small for the bond market.
Whether a loan backs a PE-owned ("sponsored") company or is made direct to an independent borrower — the split that shapes sourcing, diligence, and pricing.
Borrowing at the fund level (subscription lines, asset facilities, BDC debt) to lift a low-single-digit asset yield into a double-digit equity return — governed by asset-coverage limits.
A single blended loan that fuses senior and subordinated debt into one instrument at one blended rate — private credit's signature product, often split first-out/last-out behind the scenes.
The base rate (SOFR, post-LIBOR) over which a loan's spread is set, usually with a floor — why private credit income rises with interest rates.
The fixed premium over the reference rate that compensates the lender for credit risk and illiquidity (e.g., SOFR + 550 bps).
Funding a loan below par (e.g., at 98) so the lender earns the gap to par as extra yield — a routine pricing lever.
Terms (non-call periods, make-wholes, soft-call 101, prepayment fees) that protect a lender's yield if the borrower repays early.
Interest paid by adding to the loan balance instead of in cash — an upside feature in mezzanine, a stress signal when a healthy loan flips to PIK.
Tests of a borrower's ability to service debt from cash flow — interest coverage, fixed-charge coverage (FCCR), debt-service coverage (DSCR).
A loan sized as a percentage of an asset's value — the core risk gauge for asset-based and real-estate credit, where the asset, not cash flow, is the source of repayment.
The dynamically-sized pool of eligible collateral (receivables, inventory, equipment) against which an asset-based revolver may draw, set by advance rates.
A lender's own scorecard grading each loan's risk — the engine behind watch-lists, pricing, reserves, and (mapped to agency scales) BDC/insurance reporting.
The assets pledged to a lender and the rank of its claim — first lien, second lien, unsecured — which sets who gets paid first in a default.
The contract ranking different lenders against each other — payment and lien subordination, standstills, and the Agreement Among Lenders that splits a unitranche.
The master loan contract — the credit analogue of the SPA — setting drawdown terms, covenants, representations, and events of default.
The negotiated repair of a troubled loan outside bankruptcy — forbearance, amend-and-extend, new money, or debt-for-equity.
The downgrade machinery for deteriorating credits — moving a loan onto a watch-list and stopping income recognition when repayment is in doubt.

The two sides of every Wall Street transaction — the sell-side (banks, brokers) that advises, structures, and distributes, versus the buy-side (PE, hedge funds, asset managers) that invests; the line that defines a banking career.
category: The franchise, the product & the mandate

quick_definition: A bank's signed appointment to act for a client on a transaction, defining scope, the success fee, and exclusivity — the thing a pitch competes to win.
category: The franchise, the product & the mandate

quick_definition: The presentation a bank builds to win a mandate — market read, strategic options, a valuation view, and "why us."
category: The franchise, the product & the mandate

quick_definition: How a bank is organized — industry "coverage" bankers who own the client relationship versus "product" bankers (M&A, ECM, DCM, LevFin) who execute the deal type.
category: The franchise, the product & the mandate

quick_definition: The banker's standard trio for valuing a company — comparable companies, precedent transactions, and DCF — run together because each checks the others.
Valuing a company off the trading multiples of similar public companies — a market-based, relative valuation.
Valuing a company off the multiples paid in prior M&A deals for similar companies — relative valuation that bakes in a control premium.
Valuing a company by projecting its unlevered free cash flow and discounting it to present value at WACC — the intrinsic, cash-flow-based method.
The blended after-tax cost of a company's debt and equity, weighted by capital structure — the discount rate that pairs with unlevered free cash flow.
Cash flow from the core business available to all investors before financing — NOPAT + D&A ± change in working capital − CapEx; the numerator in a DCF.
The lump-sum value of a company's cash flows beyond the explicit forecast, via perpetuity-growth or an exit multiple — usually most of a DCF's value.
The summary chart stacking the value range from each methodology side by side, framing where a company is "worth" — the banker's headline output.
The walk between what the whole business is worth (EV) and what the shareholders' stake is worth (equity value) — adding cash, subtracting debt and other claims.
The test of whether an acquisition raises (accretive) or lowers (dilutive) the buyer's earnings per share — M&A's first-pass scorecard.
The integrated model combining buyer and target, the financing, and the synergies to project the combined company's EPS and returns.
The added value from combining two companies — cost savings and revenue gains — that justifies paying a premium.
The extra amount above a target's unaffected share price an acquirer must pay to take control — typically 10–30%, and the reason deal comps run rich.
How a buyer funds a purchase — its blend of cash on hand, new debt, and its own stock — which drives accretion/dilution and risk.
A bank's commitment to raise capital for an issuer — buying the securities to resell, taking on placement risk for a fee.
A company's first sale of shares to the public — the registration, pricing, and allocation process a bank runs (distinct from G53, the PE exit view of the same event).
The discount underwriters take between the price paid to the issuer and the price sold to investors — the bank's fee on a securities offering.
The published rankings of banks by deal volume and fees — the scoreboard that drives the franchise, pitches, and banker prestige.
A bank's formal letter that a deal's price is fair to shareholders from a financial point of view — a board's due-diligence and litigation shield.
The return a manager earns above what market exposure alone would deliver — the skill-based excess return that is the entire point of a hedge fund, and the thing it charges 20% of.
category: The objective: alpha, beta & the hedge

quick_definition: The portion of a portfolio's return that simply comes from moving with the market — the cheap, passive exposure a hedge fund tries to strip out so that what's left is alpha.
category: The objective: alpha, beta & the hedge

quick_definition: Holding offsetting long and short positions so that broad market moves cancel and the bet is on relative performance — the structural innovation (A.W. Jones, 1949) that named the industry.
category: The objective: alpha, beta & the hedge

quick_definition: Borrowing a security to sell it now and buy it back later, profiting if the price falls — the tool that lets a fund express negative views and hedge, with uniquely asymmetric risk.
category: The objective: alpha, beta & the hedge

quick_definition: Two readings of how a book is positioned: net (longs minus shorts) measures directional market bet; gross (longs plus shorts) measures total capital at work and thus leverage.
category: The objective: alpha, beta & the hedge

quick_definition: A fund's excess return (over the risk-free rate) divided by its volatility — the headline risk-adjusted return number, the way IRR is PE's and yield is private credit's.
Active return divided by active risk — the ratio of how much a manager beats their benchmark to how much tracking risk they take; the central statistic of active management.
The correlation between a manager's forecasts and what actually happens — a direct measure of skill; even a great IC is only ~0.1 (being right ~53% of the time).
The number of independent investment decisions a manager makes per year — a rate, not a holding count; the diversification term that lets a small edge compound into a high information ratio.

term: The Fundamental Law of Active Management
quick_definition: Grinold's relation IR ≈ IC · √BR · TC — that consistent outperformance requires skill × breadth × implementation efficiency; the equation behind every active strategy's design.
The two faces of hedge-fund risk: volatility (how much returns swing) and drawdown (the peak-to-trough loss) — the latter being what triggers redemptions and ends careers.
The bank service bundle a hedge fund runs on — trade execution, securities lending (to enable shorting), financing/margin (to enable leverage), custody, and capital introduction.
The peak NAV a fund must exceed before charging performance fees again — so investors never pay 20% twice on the same dollar of gains; the key nuance bolted onto the "2 and 20."
The contractual rules governing when investors can pull money out — lock-up periods, redemption notice and windows, gates, and side pockets — which must match the liquidity of what the fund trades.
The limit on how much capital a strategy can absorb before its own size erodes its returns — worsened when many managers crowd the same trade and must share that capacity.
The specific, falsifiable argument for why a security is mispriced — and crucially why the consensus is wrong (the "variant perception"); the unit of work a hedge fund produces.
category: The craft: thesis, sizing & the edge

quick_definition: The identifiable event expected to close the gap between price and value — earnings, a merger, a spin-off, a refinancing, a restructuring — that turns a cheap security into a realized gain.
category: The craft: thesis, sizing & the edge

quick_definition: The discipline of deciding how much to bet on each idea given conviction, risk, and correlation — where edge is converted into return and where most blow-ups actually happen.
category: The craft: thesis, sizing & the edge

quick_definition: Marks's idea that superior returns require thinking differently and better than the consensus — not "this is a good company" but "this is better than the market thinks, and that view isn't yet priced."
category: The craft: thesis, sizing & the edge

quick_definition: The theory that prices already reflect all information — the proposition hedge funds bet against; alpha exists only to the degree markets are inefficient, which they are, partially and unevenly.
category: The craft: thesis, sizing & the edge

term: Risk as permanent loss of capital
quick_definition: Marks's reframing of risk away from "volatility" toward the probability of a permanent, unrecoverable loss — the definition that actually governs how thoughtful investors size and hedge.
The recurring swing of markets between greed and fear, cheap and dear — never a straight line; recognizing where the pendulum sits is, for Marks, the closest thing to an edge in timing.
The flagship hedge-fund strategy: buy undervalued stocks, short overvalued ones, and earn the spread between them with reduced market exposure — the direct descendant of A.W. Jones.
category: The strategy families (each opens a Zone 2 branch)

quick_definition: Top-down investing on the direction of whole economies — expressed across currencies, rates, equity indices, and commodities — the strategy of Soros's sterling trade and the macro "rock stars."
category: The strategy families (each opens a Zone 2 branch)

quick_definition: Investing around specific corporate events — mergers (merger arbitrage), activism, spin-offs, bankruptcies (distressed) — where the return comes from the event resolving, not the market rising.
category: The strategy families (each opens a Zone 2 branch)

quick_definition: Using models, data, and code rather than discretionary judgment to find and trade many small edges at scale — the Renaissance/D.E. Shaw approach; breadth as the engine.
category: The strategy families (each opens a Zone 2 branch)

quick_definition: Running many small, independently-managed teams ("pods") under one risk umbrella and one financing arrangement — the Citadel/Millennium model that now dominates the industry's capital.
category: The strategy families (each opens a Zone 2 branch)

quick_definition: Buying the target and (often) shorting the acquirer after a deal is announced, to capture the spread between the current price and the deal price — paid for bearing the risk the deal breaks.
category: The strategy families (each opens a Zone 2 branch)

quick_definition: Taking a meaningful stake in a public company and using it to push for change — board seats, strategy, capital return, a sale — to force the value gap closed rather than waiting for it.
category: The strategy families (each opens a Zone 2 branch)

quick_definition: The hedge-fund mandate of making money in any market environment, measured against zero (or cash), rather than beating a benchmark index — the promise that distinguishes it from long-only.
category: The objective: alpha, beta & the hedge

quick_definition: The durable reason a manager can profit where others can't — better information, better analysis, better behavior, or better structure; without an articulable edge, returns are just luck or beta.
category: The craft: thesis, sizing & the edge

quick_definition: The split that defines the discipline: passive investing buys and holds the whole market (an index) at minimal cost and accepts the market return; active investing tries to beat a benchmark by selecting securities or timing, and charges more for the attempt.
category: The defining split: passive, the index fund & the ETF

quick_definition: A fund that mechanically holds every security in a market index in its exact weights, delivering the index's return minus a tiny fee — the vehicle that turned "buy the market" from a theory into the dominant product.
category: The defining split: passive, the index fund & the ETF

quick_definition: An exchange-traded fund: an index (or active) portfolio that trades intraday like a stock, kept in line with its underlying value by authorized participants who create and redeem shares in-kind against the basket — the mechanism behind its low cost and tax efficiency.
category: The defining split: passive, the index fund & the ETF

quick_definition: The index a portfolio is measured against — the yardstick that defines "the market" for a given mandate, sets the manager's neutral starting weights, and turns return into relative return.

How far a portfolio's returns swing away from its benchmark: tracking error is the volatility of the return gap (the active-risk budget a manager spends), while tracking difference is the realized gap itself (mostly fees, for an index fund).

The percentage of a portfolio that differs from its benchmark's holdings — a measure of how genuinely active a manager is, and the tool that exposes the "closet indexer" who charges active fees for near-benchmark holdings.

The decision of how much to put in each asset class — strategic (the long-run policy mix that drives most of a portfolio's return and risk) versus tactical (short-run tilts away from it) — the single most consequential choice an investor makes.

The written long-term target allocation to each asset class that an institution commits to and rebalances back toward — the anchor that turns an investment philosophy into a disciplined, repeatable portfolio.

term: Modern Portfolio Theory & the efficient frontier
quick_definition: Markowitz's framework: by combining assets that don't move together, an investor can build portfolios that maximize expected return for a given level of risk — the set of those best portfolios is the efficient frontier.

Holding many imperfectly-correlated assets so that idiosyncratic risks cancel and only systematic risk remains — "the only free lunch in investing," and the engine that makes a multi-asset portfolio less risky than its parts.

Periodically selling what has risen and buying what has fallen to bring a portfolio back to its policy weights — the discipline that enforces "buy low, sell high," controls risk drift, and harvests a small return premium.

Rules-based strategies that tilt a portfolio toward documented return factors — value, size, momentum, quality, low-volatility — packaged as cheap, transparent index-like products; the middle ground between passive and active.

The classification of equity managers and funds by what kind of stocks they hold — growth vs. value on one axis, large- vs. mid- vs. small-cap on the other — the grid (popularized by Morningstar) that organizes the active-equity world.

The compounding insight at the heart of indexing: because active management is a zero-sum game before costs, the average active dollar must underperform the index by the amount of its fees and trading costs — and that gap, compounded over decades, is enormous.
category: Cost, fees & the manager as a business

quick_definition: The asset manager's business model: revenue is a small percentage of assets under management, so profit is driven by gathering and retaining scale rather than by performance — a fundamentally different engine from the hedge fund's 2-and-20.
category: Cost, fees & the manager as a business

quick_definition: The classic retail vehicle: an open-end fund that issues and redeems shares daily at net asset value (NAV), pools money from many small investors, and is heavily regulated for investor protection — the workhorse of mass-market asset management.

A portfolio a manager runs for a single large client (a pension, an endowment, an insurer) under a negotiated mandate — the client owns the underlying securities directly, with custom guidelines, fees, and reporting; the institutional counterpart to a pooled fund.

Swensen's approach to running perpetual institutional capital: an equity-oriented, heavily-diversified policy portfolio that leans into illiquid alternatives (private equity, hedge funds, real assets) to harvest an illiquidity premium, executed through carefully-selected external managers — the seat of the allocator who funds every other module.

The legal and ethical obligation to manage other people's money solely in their interest, with the care a prudent professional would use — the constraint that shapes everything a long-only manager does, from diversification requirements to the ban on reckless concentration.

term: Manager selection (the allocator's manager due diligence)
quick_definition: How an institution decides which external managers to hire — judging track record, process, team, edge, and fees to identify rare persistent skill ex ante; the public-markets sibling of GP due diligence, and the allocator's hardest problem given Sharpe's arithmetic.

The long-only mandate's definition of success: beating (or matching) a benchmark index, measured against the market rather than against zero — the structural opposite of the hedge fund's absolute-return promise, and the reason tracking error, not drawdown, is the operative risk.
category: The capstone: relative return & the debate

quick_definition: The defining argument of modern asset management: whether paying for active management is worth it, given that most active managers underperform their benchmark after fees — the capstone question that fee compression, the rise of indexing, and the survival of genuine skill all turn on.
category: The capstone: relative return & the debate

quick_definition: The comprehensive, ongoing process of organizing a household's whole financial life — cash flow, saving, investing, insurance, taxes, retirement, and estate — into a single coordinated plan aimed at the family's goals, of which investing is only one part.

Structuring a portfolio around a household's specific, dated goals (retirement, a home, education, legacy) rather than around beating a benchmark — often by carving wealth into separate "buckets," each with its own time horizon, risk level, and asset mix.

term: The advisor–client relationship & behavioral coaching ("advisor's alpha")
quick_definition: The core of the wealth-management product: a trusted advisor who plans, allocates, and — crucially — coaches the client through fear and greed, where the largest measurable value an advisor adds is often behavioral (stopping the client from buying high and selling low), not investment selection.

The present value of a person's future earnings — the largest asset on most households' balance sheets early in life, usually bond-like in risk, which depletes as it is converted into financial capital over a working life and reshapes how much investment risk a person can bear.

Two distinct things often conflated: risk tolerance is how much volatility a client can emotionally stomach (a preference); risk capacity is how much risk their goals and balance sheet can actually withstand (a constraint) — and a prudent plan is governed by the lower of the two.

An individual's finite, shifting investment horizon — and the glide path that follows from it: a planned schedule that reduces portfolio risk as a goal (usually retirement) approaches, the individual's policy portfolio that moves with age.

The framework that treats investing as a multi-decade arc shaped by age, human capital, and goals — accumulate aggressively when young and human-capital-rich, de-risk and decumulate in retirement — the theory beneath target-date funds and glide paths.

Managing a portfolio for after-tax return — the only return a taxable household actually keeps — most importantly by asset location: placing tax-inefficient assets (bonds, REITs, high-turnover strategies) in tax-sheltered accounts and tax-efficient assets (index equities) in taxable ones.

Deliberately realizing investment losses to offset taxable gains (and a limited amount of ordinary income), then reinvesting in a similar-but-not-identical holding to stay invested — a systematic way to convert market volatility into a recurring tax benefit.

The account types that shelter investment growth from tax — traditional (tax-deferred) and Roth (tax-free) IRAs and 401(k)s, plus 529s, HSAs, and others — whose contribution limits, tax treatment, and withdrawal rules shape the entire household saving-and-decumulation plan.

The under-studied second half of investing: turning an accumulated nest egg into a reliable lifetime income — deciding how much to withdraw, from which accounts, in what order, without running out of money or leaving too much unspent.

The planning heuristic that a retiree can withdraw roughly 4% of the initial portfolio (inflation-adjusted thereafter) with a high probability of not outliving the money over ~30 years — a rule of thumb, fiercely debated, that anchors decumulation planning.

The danger, unique to decumulation, that the order of returns matters: poor returns in the early retirement years — while withdrawals are draining the portfolio — can exhaust it even if the long-run average return is fine, because there is less capital left to recover.

Longevity risk is the risk of outliving one's money; annuitization is the main tool against it — converting a lump sum into a guaranteed lifetime income stream (a personal pension), trading liquidity and bequest for the certainty of never running out.

Arranging in advance how a person's assets and affairs will be managed at incapacity and transferred at death — through wills, trusts, powers of attorney, healthcare directives, and beneficiary designations — to direct the transfer, minimize tax and probate, and prevent family conflict.

A legal arrangement in which a trustee holds and manages assets for beneficiaries under the terms a grantor sets — the central instrument of estate planning, used to avoid probate, control how and when wealth passes, protect assets, and reduce transfer taxes.

The movement of wealth from one generation to the next — the gift-and-estate-tax system, the generation-skipping transfer tax, the strategies (gifting, trusts, valuation discounts) used to pass more of it intact, and the family-governance work of preparing heirs to receive it.

The bundled, relationship-based service banks offer wealthy clients — combining banking, bespoke lending (against securities, art, or property), and investment management under one dedicated relationship manager — the high-touch channel for high-net-worth households.

A private organization that manages the entire financial life of a single ultra-wealthy family (a single-family office) or several families (a multi-family office) — investments, taxes, estate, philanthropy, governance, and concierge needs — the most bespoke and integrated form of wealth management.

An automated platform that builds and maintains a diversified, low-cost portfolio for an individual algorithmically — onboarding, allocation, rebalancing, and tax-loss harvesting with little or no human advisor — the technology-driven democratization of basic wealth management.

Owning the individual securities of an index directly in a separately managed account (rather than via a fund), so the holdings can be personalized and — most importantly — tax-loss-harvested at the single-stock level, bringing institutional tax techniques down to the individual.

How advice is paid for — a percentage of assets under management (the dominant model), flat or hourly fee-only planning, commission on products sold, or a fee-based hybrid — each carrying different incentives and conflicts that the client must understand.

The defining standard-of-care question in advice: a fiduciary must act in the client's best interest, while a (historically lower) suitability standard required only that a product be "suitable" — the distinction that determines whose interest comes first when advisor and client incentives diverge.

term: The research report (the note & the initiation)
quick_definition: The written, distributed artifact in which an analyst states a rating, a price target, and the reasoning — the unit in which sell-side research is actually delivered.
category: Group A — The published research product

quick_definition: The analyst's living spreadsheet that projects a company's financial statements forward to produce the numbers every other output depends on.
category: Group B — The analyst's linked outputs (the five-task chain made concrete)

quick_definition: A specific forward forecast (EPS, revenue, EBITDA) for a company, and the average of all analysts' estimates against which surprise and revision are measured.
category: Group B — The analyst's linked outputs (the five-task chain made concrete)

quick_definition: A published, time-bounded estimate of where a stock's price should go, derived from the model and the chosen valuation method.
category: Group B — The analyst's linked outputs (the five-task chain made concrete)

quick_definition: The discrete, published recommendation that translates the gap between price target and current price into an instruction.
category: Group B — The analyst's linked outputs (the five-task chain made concrete)

quick_definition: The analyst's reasoned, named-and-distributed argument for why a stock will move — the same logical object as the internal thesis, but published, attributed, and career-bearing.
category: Group B — The analyst's linked outputs (the five-task chain made concrete)

quick_definition: The continual process of raising or lowering estimates as new information arrives — itself one of the most-watched signals in markets.
category: Group C — Coverage & the information edge

quick_definition: The defined set of companies an analyst follows (typically one sector), the basis of the analyst's expertise and the boundary of their authority.
category: Group C — Coverage & the information edge

quick_definition: The practice of building an information edge from many individually-immaterial, lawful data points — the legitimate counterpart to trading on inside information.
category: Group C — Coverage & the information edge

quick_definition: The few variables that actually determine a stock's performance — Valentine's signature idea that an analyst's whole job is finding and monitoring the 2% that matters.
category: Group C — Coverage & the information edge

term: The quarterly earnings cycle (the print, the call, the update)
aliases: the print, the call, the update
quick_definition: The recurring rhythm — results release, management call, model update, note — around which the entire research calendar is organized.
The gap between reported results and consensus, and the forward expectations management itself sets — the twin events that move stocks at the print.
Research produced inside an asset manager or hedge fund for its own portfolio decisions, never published, judged only by returns.
category: Group E — The branches (who else does this, and who pays)

quick_definition: Research from providers with no banking or brokerage conflict, increasingly built on alternative datasets and machine analysis rather than the traditional analyst note.
category: Group E — The branches (who else does this, and who pays)

term: The research franchise & analyst rankings (the II survey)
quick_definition: The reputational capital of a sell-side analyst — historically measured by the Institutional Investor poll — that determines client access, compensation, and influence.
category: Group F — The franchise & the economics

term: How research is paid for (the bundle, soft dollars & MiFID II unbundling)
aliases: the bundle, soft dollars & MiFID II unbundling
quick_definition: The economics of the research product: historically bundled into trading commissions and soft dollars, then forcibly unbundled in Europe by MiFID II.
category: Group F — The franchise & the economics

quick_definition: The three-desk structure of a sell-side equities division and the flows of ideas, orders, and commissions that bind research to the rest of the firm.
category: Group F — The franchise & the economics

term: The research/banking conflict & the Global Research Settlement (post-Spitzer)
quick_definition: The structural conflict between objective research and investment-banking revenue, and the 2003 settlement that walled them apart.
category: Group G — The regulatory & conflict frame

term: Regulation AC & the disclosure regime (Reg AC + Reg FD + quiet periods)
aliases: Reg AC + Reg FD + quiet periods
quick_definition: The rules requiring analysts to certify their own views, barring selective corporate disclosure, and silencing research around deals.
category: Group G — The regulatory & conflict frame

quick_definition: The venture return distribution in which a tiny number of investments produce nearly all of a fund's returns — the empirical law that dictates how VC is sourced, sized, and managed.
category: Group A — VC's organizing logic & returns

quick_definition: The discipline of building a portfolio of bets each sized to be capable of returning the entire fund on its own, using ownership targets rather than diversification logic.
category: Group A — VC's organizing logic & returns

quick_definition: The convention of carrying a holding at its most recent round price, producing paper gains (TVPI) that may diverge sharply from realized cash (DPI) until exit actually occurs.
category: Group A — VC's organizing logic & returns

quick_definition: The sequence of discrete financing events (pre-seed → seed → Series A/B/C → growth) that take a startup from idea to scale, each funding the company to its next value-creating milestone.
category: Group B — The financing structure (the round)

quick_definition: The round-pricing identity (post-money = pre-money + new investment) that determines how much ownership new capital buys — the single most consequential arithmetic in a venture financing.
category: Group B — The financing structure (the round)

quick_definition: Early-stage instruments that accept money now and defer pricing to a future priced round, bridged by a valuation cap and/or a discount rate.
category: Group B — The financing structure (the round)

term: The option pool & the pre-money shuffle
quick_definition: The employee equity reserve (ESOP), and the VC convention of expanding it inside the pre-money valuation so that the dilution falls on existing shareholders rather than new investors.
category: Group B — The financing structure (the round)

quick_definition: The independent third-party valuation of a private company's common stock that sets the strike price for employee options — typically well below the preferred-share round price.
category: Group B — The financing structure (the round)

quick_definition: The contractual right to invest in later rounds to maintain ownership percentage, combined with the fund-level discipline of reserving capital to double down on the portfolio's winners.
A financing priced below the prior round, triggering anti-dilution protections, reordering the liquidation-preference stack, and often damaging morale and the cap table simultaneously.

term: VC governance: board seats, information rights & protective provisions
quick_definition: The minority-investor control toolkit — influence exercised through board representation and veto rights rather than through the majority-control ownership of a buyout.
The top of the venture funnel — generating and accessing investable opportunities — where the power law makes seeing the best deals as decisive as picking well among average ones.
category: Group D — Sourcing, the founder & the company's clock

quick_definition: Underwriting the team over the financials at the earliest stages — "the jockey, not the horse" — as the central and often dominant judgment of early-stage venture investing.
category: Group D — Sourcing, the founder & the company's clock

quick_definition: The startup's cash-consumption rate and the number of months of survival that current cash buys — the company's clock, driving every financing decision and every milestone target.
category: Group D — Sourcing, the founder & the company's clock

quick_definition: Late-stage pre-IPO venture in which public-market investors (hedge funds, mutual funds) cross into private financing rounds, blurring the boundary between private and public capital.
The VC firm as institution — the partnership structure, its principals, scouts, and platform team that source, win, and support deals beyond the check itself.

term: The four quadrants of real estate
quick_definition: The 2×2 map of the investable universe — public vs. private × debt vs. equity — that organizes every way capital touches real estate (REITs, CMBS, private funds, commercial mortgages).
category: Group A — The asset and how it is valued (the foundational frame)

quick_definition: A property's annual income after operating expenses but before debt service, capital expenditures, and income tax — the cash-earnings engine that every real-estate valuation runs on.
category: Group A — The asset and how it is valued (the foundational frame)

term: The capitalization rate (cap rate) & direct capitalization
quick_definition: The ratio of NOI to value (cap rate = NOI ÷ value) that prices income property the way a P/E multiple prices a stock — the central real-estate valuation metric.
category: Group A — The asset and how it is valued (the foundational frame)

quick_definition: The three lenses for valuing a property — income (cap rate/DCF), sales comparison (comps), and cost (replacement cost less depreciation) — the real-estate counterpart to IB's three methodologies.
category: Group A — The asset and how it is valued (the foundational frame)

quick_definition: The classification of real estate by use — office, retail, industrial, multifamily, hospitality, plus the alternative/specialty sectors — each with its own demand drivers, lease structure, and cycle.
category: Group B — Sectors, styles and vehicles

quick_definition: The four-point spectrum — core, core-plus, value-add, opportunistic — that sorts real-estate strategies by leverage, the income-vs.-appreciation mix, and how much work the business plan requires.
category: Group B — Sectors, styles and vehicles

quick_definition: The tax-pass-through vehicle that owns income property and must distribute ≥90% of taxable income, paying no entity-level tax — the public-equity quadrant of real estate.
category: Group B — Sectors, styles and vehicles

term: Open-end vs. closed-end real estate funds
quick_definition: The structural split between perpetual-life core funds (open-end, with queues and redemptions) and finite-life value-add/opportunistic funds (closed-end, the PE structure) — a distinction unique to real estate among private-capital vehicles.
category: Group B — Sectors, styles and vehicles

quick_definition: The deal-level partnership between an operating partner (the sponsor, who finds and manages the deal) and a capital partner (who provides most of the equity), aligned by the promote.
category: Group B — Sectors, styles and vehicles

quick_definition: The layered financing of a real-estate deal — senior debt, mezzanine, preferred equity, common equity — ranked by priority of payment and inverse to risk and return.
The senior loan secured by income property (amortization, balloon, recourse vs. non-recourse), and the securitization of those loans into commercial mortgage-backed securities sold to bond investors.

The multi-year hold model that projects NOI, capital expenditure, debt service, and cash flow, ending in the reversion (exit value = stabilized NOI ÷ exit cap rate) — the document where the exit-cap assumption dominates the return.
The contract that converts a building into an income stream — gross vs. net (single/double/triple-net) vs. modified-gross — defining who pays which expenses and therefore what NOI actually is.
category: Group D — Operations, leasing and development

quick_definition: The operational health of a property — occupancy, vacancy, absorption, the rent roll, lease rollover, and the tenant-improvement and leasing-commission (TI/LC) costs that sit below NOI — the asset manager's core dashboard.
category: Group D — Operations, leasing and development

quick_definition: Ground-up creation of property — site acquisition → entitlement → design → construction (financed by loan-to-cost draws) → lease-up → stabilization — the highest-risk, highest-return real-estate activity.
category: Group D — Operations, leasing and development

quick_definition: The physical property cycle — recovery → expansion → hypersupply → recession — driven by the lag between demand and the construction pipeline, distinct from (and interacting with) the capital-markets cycle.
category: Group E — Cycle, tax and the public-market lens

quick_definition: The tax machinery that lifts after-tax real-estate returns — depreciation (and accelerated cost segregation), the 1031 like-kind exchange, opportunity zones, and the depreciation-recapture cost at sale.
category: Group E — Cycle, tax and the public-market lens

quick_definition: Funds from operations — net income with real-estate depreciation added back — and its capex-adjusted refinement AFFO; the cash-earnings measures used to value REITs because GAAP net income is distorted by depreciation.
category: Group E — Cycle, tax and the public-market lens

quick_definition: The net asset value of a REIT's underlying property less its debt, and the premium or discount at which the REIT's traded shares diverge from that value — the arbitrage signal linking public and private real-estate pricing.
category: Group E — Cycle, tax and the public-market lens

quick_definition: The total market value of all final goods and services an economy produces in a period — the single headline measure of how big an economy is and whether it is growing or shrinking.
The recurring expansion-peak-recession-trough fluctuation of real economic output around its long-run trend — the rhythm of boom and bust in the real economy.
A broad, sustained contraction in economic activity (a depression is a rare, severe, prolonged one) — usually a shortfall of demand rather than a loss of productive capacity.

term: Aggregate demand, aggregate supply & the output gap (Z1.8)
quick_definition: Total economy-wide spending vs. total productive capacity, and the gap between actual output and potential output that drives inflation and unemployment.
The share of people who want work but can't find it, its types (frictional, structural, cyclical), and its link to output via Okun's law — half of the Fed's mandate.
The dashboard of data — payrolls, PMIs, jobless claims, retail sales, housing starts — that signals where the cycle is and where it's heading.
The government's lever — taxes and spending — used to stabilize the economy, and the deficits and accumulated debt that result.
A medium of exchange, store of value, and unit of account whose modern form ("fiat") has value only because the government declares it legal tender.

term: The central bank & the Federal Reserve (Z2.2)
quick_definition: The institution that issues money and sets monetary policy; in the US, the Fed — with its FOMC and its dual mandate of stable prices and maximum employment.
The central bank's management of money and interest rates to steer inflation and employment — easing (stimulating) or tightening (restraining).
The total money in the economy (M1, M2) and how commercial banks create most of it by lending — the quantity side of monetary policy.

term: The federal funds rate (the policy rate) (Z2.5)
quick_definition: The overnight interest rate the Fed targets — the anchor from which nearly every other interest rate in the economy is set.

term: The risk-free rate & the term structure of interest rates (Z2.6)
quick_definition: The return on default-free government debt across maturities — the baseline "price of time" that anchors the discount rate for every other asset (the home node referenced inside WACC G91).

term: The real vs. nominal interest rate (the Fisher relation) (Z2.7)
quick_definition: The distinction between the stated (nominal) rate and the inflation-adjusted (real) rate — what a lender actually earns and a borrower actually pays in purchasing power.
The chain by which a change in the policy rate flows through borrowing costs, asset prices, credit, the exchange rate, and confidence to reach the real economy.
The central bank's creation of reserves to buy bonds (QE) — and the reversal (QT) — used to ease or tighten when the policy rate alone is not enough.
A sustained rise (inflation) or fall (deflation) in the general price level — the erosion or growth of money's purchasing power over time.
The baskets used to measure inflation — consumer prices (CPI), the Fed's preferred PCE deflator, and producer prices (PPI) — and the headline-vs-core distinction.
The short-run tradeoff between inflation and unemployment that becomes vertical in the long run at the "natural rate" — the core framework behind every Fed decision.
The pro-cyclical expansion and contraction of lending, leverage, and credit availability — the macro engine behind deal volume, entry multiples, and default waves.

term: The debt cycle (short-term & long-term / Big Debt Cycle) (Z4.2)
quick_definition: Dalio's framework of debt accumulating relative to income — a short-term version (≈ the business cycle) nested inside a long-term ≈80-year cycle that ends in deleveraging.

term: The yield curve & its shape (the inversion signal) (Z4.5)
quick_definition: The plot of government-bond yields across maturities; its slope (normal, flat, inverted) is the market's most-watched signal of the cycle and of coming recessions.
The overall ease or tightness of money — rates, credit spreads, the exchange rate, and asset prices taken together — that governs deal activity and valuations ("the rate environment").

term: The discount-rate channel & the equity risk premium (CAPM bridge) (Z4.7)
quick_definition: The mechanism by which the risk-free rate plus a risk premium set the discount rate that prices every asset — the reason multiples compress and cap rates rise when rates rise (homes the ERP/CAPM referenced inside WACC G91).
The price of one currency in another, the regimes that govern it (floating, fixed, pegged), and what makes it move (rate differentials, flows, trade).

term: The balance of payments (trade, current & capital accounts) (Z5.3)
quick_definition: The accounting of a country's transactions with the world — the trade balance, the current account, and the offsetting capital flows.
Government borrowing and the risk it isn't repaid — through outright default, restructuring, or inflation — including "debt intolerance" and serial default.
The recurring varieties of crisis (banking, currency, inflation, sovereign-default) and how they cluster, spread, and follow the "this time is different" pattern.
A tradable IOU — a promise to pay fixed coupons on set dates and return the face value at maturity — the atomic unit of the fixed-income market.

term: Yield to maturity (& the meanings of yield) (Z2.5)
quick_definition: The single discount rate that equates a bond's price to the PV of its remaining cash flows — the market's quoted "yield," one of several yield concepts that must never be conflated.
A bond's price is the present value of its cash flows, which makes price and yield move inversely — the mechanical reason bond prices fall when rates rise.
The sensitivity of a bond's price to a change in yield — the master risk number of fixed income (DV01 is the same idea in dollars per basis point).
The curvature in the price–yield relationship — why duration understates gains and overstates losses, and why prepayable bonds can have negative convexity.

term: Spot, forward & par rates (the curve toolkit) (Z2.7)
quick_definition: The pricing machinery extracted from bond prices — discount factors, zero-coupon spot rates, implied forwards, and par yields — the arithmetic form of the term structure.
The market for U.S. government debt — bills, notes, and bonds — the largest, most liquid bond market on earth and the risk-free benchmark everything else is priced against.
Treasuries whose principal indexes to CPI, paying a real yield — and, via the gap to nominal yields, the market's own inflation forecast (the breakeven).
The market for debt maturing inside a year — T-bills, commercial paper, certificates of deposit, fed funds — where the policy rate becomes actual funding.
State and local government debt whose interest is federally tax-exempt — compared to taxable bonds via the taxable-equivalent yield, the core math of the retail bond buyer.
Standardized, rated, tradable debt issued by companies under an indenture — the public-market sibling of the private loan and the output of IB's debt capital markets.

term: Investment grade vs. high yield (the ratings divide) (Z4.3)
quick_definition: The BBB−/Baa3 line that splits the bond market into two regimes — who may own the paper, which index it joins, what it costs to issue — and the "fallen angel" dynamics at the boundary.
The public letter-grade scales (Moody's, S&P, Fitch) that price default risk into markets — with hard-wired consequences for index membership, mandates, and funding costs.
The extra yield a bond offers over the Treasury curve — measured against a point (G), the whole curve (Z), or net of embedded options (OAS) — the market's running price of credit risk.
A contract that pays out if a borrower defaults, in exchange for a running premium — tradable default insurance that lets credit risk be bought, sold, and measured apart from the bond.
The machine that pools illiquid loans (mortgages, cards, autos), routes their cash flows through a trust, and issues tradable bonds in tranches against the pool.
Bonds backed by pools of government-guaranteed home mortgages, whose defining risk is not default but prepayment — homeowners refinancing exactly when investors least want it.
Sovereign and corporate debt of developing economies, split into local-currency bonds (rates + FX risk) and hard-currency bonds (credit risk) — a distinct asset class with its own indices.
Selling a security with an agreement to buy it back — a collateralized loan that finances every bond desk, sets the secured overnight rate (SOFR's raw material), and transmits leverage system-wide.

term: The bond index & the benchmark (the Agg) (Z5.5)
quick_definition: The rules-based baskets (Bloomberg Aggregate, EMBI, HY indices) that define "the bond market" for portfolios — with the quirk that issuers get bigger weights by owing more.
Structuring a bond portfolio so assets move with liabilities — matching duration so a pension or insurer is hedged against rates rather than betting on them.
Bonds carrying issuer or holder options (call, put, conversion) whose value the market strips out via yield-to-worst and OAS — the reason a 6% callable isn't a 6% bullet.
Net interest income divided by average earning assets — how a bank makes money on the gap between what it lends at and pays for.

term: Regulatory capital: CET1, RWAs & the Basel ratios (Z5.1)
quick_definition: The equity cushion a bank must hold — common-equity Tier 1 as a share of risk-weighted assets, plus buffers — the sector binding rule.
Policyholder money held between premium and claim — an investable pool whose cost is the underwriting result.
Claims plus expenses as a share of premiums; under 100% means the insurer was paid to hold the float.

term: Provision for loan losses & the allowance (Z3.1)
quick_definition: The estimated charge for loans expected to go bad (provision) and the reserve it feeds (allowance) — where bank earnings become a judgment.
Loans no longer paying as agreed, and the ratios (NPLs, charge-offs, coverage) that test whether the loan book is as healthy as claimed.
The self-fulfilling withdrawal stampede that is banking signature failure, and the government guarantee that stops it — at the cost of moral hazard.
